title: Rapid multiplexed qPCR analysis of cerebrospinal fluid and blood for highly prevalent somatic alterations present in central nervous system tumors
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"rhlge","text":"This protocol describes a qPCR-based assay that is designed to detect twelve potential mutations from clinical specimens that are significant to CNS neoplasms. The assay is optimized for plasma and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), where both cell-free DNA (cfDNA) and DNA derived from circulating tumor cells (CTCs) can be analyzed. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c63dg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"mro5y","text":"The low yields of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) studies and serologies in current diagnostic workflows for suspected neoplasms often lead to surgical biopsies of high-risk lesions, particularly in the central nervous system (CNS). To address this need, we developed an assay to rapidly identify hallmark mutations of neoplasms in CSF and plasma that have the potential to facilitate diagnosis, treatment monitoring, and detection of recurrence (Gupta et al. Blood 2021). Here we describe the qPCR-based approach to rapidly detect hallmark point mutations in TERT promoter, IDH1/2, BRAF, MYD88, and H3F3A genes. The reaction protocol is adapted from previous descriptions (Gupta et al. Blood 2021; Shankar et al. PNAS 2018, Shankar et al. JAMA Oncol 2015), with further updates for improved sensitivity and specificity. This assay can be performed on plasma or CSF to analyze both cfDNA as well as DNA derived from circulating tumor cells (CTCs). Variant alleles can be detected from the liquid biopsies within 80 minutes.  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lhfrf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Collect CSF in 15mL Falcon tube. Collect blood in BD Vacutainer PPT Plasma Preparation Tube.
Centrifuge CSF at 600 xg, 10 minutes at 4 o C. Centrifuge blood at 1100 xg, 10 minutes at 20 o C.
Gently transfer the CSF supernatant, cell pellet, and plasma fraction to separate 1.5 mL Eppendorf tubes.

Perform DNA extractions using the respective Qiagen extraction kits (Figure 5) using manufacturer-provided instructions and reagents. Extract from a minimum of 2mL of plasma and 5mL of CSF to obtain optimal DNA yield.
Modification to manufacturer-provided kit protocols: elute the final cell-free DNA (from plasma or CSF) into 35 µL of Ultraclean water and cellular pellet DNA (from CSF) into 50 µL of Buffer AE.
Determine the appropriate quantities and concentrations for the necessary reagents (Figure 7).
Prepare reaction master mixes with the required ingredients (Figure 1).
Add 7 µL of master mix to each reaction well in a 96-well plate - positive controls, negative controls, no template controls, and samples (Figure 2)
Add 5 ng of control DNA to designated wells and fill those control wells to a final volume of 10 µL.
Add 3 µL of DNA extracts to the wells designated for samples.
Cover qPCR plate with Optical Adhesive PCR film.
Centrifuge plate for 5-10 seconds on a tabletop centrifuge and keep on ice, away from light.
Place plate in qPCR machine and run protocol (Figure 3).
Analyze data to assess the significance of the findings (Figure 4). Data analysis software may vary.

10.1001/jamaoncol.2015.0917 A rapid genotyping panel for detection of primary central nervous system lymphoma by Mihir Gupta, Evan J. Burns, Nicholas Z. Georgantas, Julia Thierauf, Naema Nayyar, Amanda Gordon, SooAe S. Jones, Michelle Pisapia, Ying Sun, Ryan P. Burns, Jose Velarde, Justin T. Jordan, Matthew J. Frigault, Brian V. Nahed, Pamela S. Jones, Fred G. Barker, William T. Curry, Rajiv Gupta, Tracy T. Batchelor, Javier M. Romero, Priscilla K. Brastianos, Hetal D. Marble, Maria Martinez-Lage, Kensuke Tateishi, Jochen K. Lennerz, Jorg Dietrich, Daniel P. Cahill, Bob S. Carter, Ganesh M. Shankar published in Blood on 18 Mar, 2021 https://dx.doi.org/10.1182/blood.2020010137

Figure 1. Figure 1. Master Mix Recipes . Master Mix reagents and concentrations vary based on the target gene. All reactions require GAPDH oligos and polymerase. A working stock concentration of 25uM is recommended for all custom oligos. Abbreviations: LNA, locked nucleic acid (5’ nuclease) probe; PNA, peptide nucleic acid. Figure 2. Figure 2. qPCR plate setup . Master Mixes are prepared in Eppendorf tubes. The full TetRS panel can be performed on a single 96-well plate as shown. All reactions include the following: positive controls for the mutations on interest, a negative control, a no template control(NTC), and DNA extracts from CSF and Plasma samples. Figure 3. Figure 3. qPCR protocol . This protocol is typically run on the Applied Biosystems Quantstudio 7 Pro Real-Team PCR System. The specific fluorescents and quenchers must be selected on the device so that the fluorescence signals can be analyzed properly.&nbsp; Figure 4. Figure 4. Analysis . Mutation detection is determined by a qPCR fluorescence signal that appears on the amplification plot. A) Clear negatives where no mutant signal was detected, and sufficient DNA was present. B) Top image shows a small level of detection but would not be sufficient to indicate a true positive result. The bottom image indicates insufficient DNA based on GAPDH detection and would not be interpretable. C) Positive detections of a mutant signal. D) Interpretation of complete results. Figure 5. Figure 5. General workflow . The full protocol following fluid collection can be completed within 80 minutes, allowing for a much faster result compared to larger molecular panels. Length of time may vary depending on equipment availability and the experience of personnel. *Astricts indicate steps where, after which, samples can be stored, and the experiment can be finished later. Figure 6. Table 1. Primer sequences. 5’ nuclease probes selectively bind to mutant alleles via the incorporation of modified locked nucleic acids (designated by a preceding “+”). Selected reverse primers contained modified thymine bases designed to stabilize oligonucleotide hybridization (designated by a preceding “*”). Table adapted from a prior report with permission of the authors; all sequences have been updated to reflect reagents utilized in this study. Abbreviations: LNA, locked nucleic acid (5’ nuclease) probe; PNA, peptide nucleic acid; sSNV, somatic single nucleotide variant. Figure 7. Table 2. Reagent concentrations. All concentrations are displayed in nanomolar. Table adapted from a prior report with permission of the authors; all values have been updated to reflect reagent concentrations utilized in this study. Abbreviations: LNA, locked nucleic acid (5’ nuclease) probe; PNA, peptide nucleic acid; sSNV, somatic single nucleotide variant.
